title: Direct observation of surface bandgap shrinkage and negative electronic compressibility in SrTiO3
authors: Warakorn Jindata, Trung-Phuc Vo, Chutchawan Jaisuk, Sung-Kwan Mo, Thanh-Tien Nguyen, Ján Minár, Worawat Meevasana
abstract: In this work, we investigate and compare the electronic structures of SrTiO3 and KTaO3 under ultraviolet (UV) light induced electron doping. Using angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), the evolution of the surface electronic structures of SrTiO3 and KTaO3 is systematically examined as a function of electron density. In contrast to KTaO3, SrTiO3 exhibits a pronounced shrinking of its surface bandgap by approximately 390 meV, accompanied by a counterintuitive shift of the valence band peak toward lower binding energies of up to 200 meV with increasing electron density. This anomalous behavior constitutes a spectroscopic signature of negative electronic compressibility (NEC). Density-functional-theory calculations provide qualitative support for the experimental observations. The calculations show that surface formation already reduces the apparent near-gap separation in SrTiO3, while additional electron accumulation further drives the slab toward a more metallic state; oxygen-vacancy models likewise produce strong bandgap reduction, identifying plausible mechanisms contributing to the observed surface bandgap shrinkage. These findings establish a direct spectroscopic link between bandgap engineering and the NEC effect at the SrTiO3 surface, highlighting the potential of SrTiO3 for next-generation oxide electronic, optoelectronic, and high-performance capacitive energy storage devices applications.
summary: This paper reports the direct observation of surface bandgap shrinkage and negative electronic compressibility in SrTiO3 using ARPES. While KTaO3 shows conventional band-filling behavior, SrTiO3 exhibits an anomalous shift in the valence band that reduces the bandgap as electron density increases. DFT calculations suggest this is driven by surface formation and electron accumulation. These findings are significant for developing next-generation oxide-based electronics and high-performance energy storage devices.
main_problem: The study investigates the anomalous evolution of the surface bandgap and the presence of negative electronic compressibility (NEC) in SrTiO3 under electron doping.
main_result: SrTiO3 exhibits a pronounced surface bandgap shrinkage of ~390 meV and a counterintuitive valence band shift toward lower binding energies, serving as a spectroscopic signature of NEC, unlike the conventional behavior seen in KTaO3.
method: The researchers used Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy (ARPES) to measure electronic structures and Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations to model surface slabs and oxygen vacancies.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of a two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) formed at the surfaces of single-crystal SrTiO3 and KTaO3 via UV light-induced electron doping.
key_observables: Surface bandgap (onset-to-onset and peak-to-peak), valence band peak position, Fermi momentum (kF), and chemical potential shift.
important_parameters: Electron density (n2D) up to 7.70 x 10^13 cm^-2, photon energies (45-65 eV), and binding energy.
main_assumptions: The oxygen vacancy models use a high vacancy concentration in a small supercell, which may overestimate the effect compared to the dilute-defect limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares bandgap evolution in SrTiO3 and KTaO3; Figure 4-6 show DFT-calculated band structures for bulk, slab, and oxygen-vacancy models.
paper_structure: The paper begins with experimental ARPES characterization of the 2DEG, compares the electronic response of SrTiO3 to KTaO3, provides DFT-based mechanistic explanations for bandgap shrinkage, and concludes with implications for device applications.
why_interesting: The observation of negative electronic compressibility and the resulting anomalous band-filling behavior provides insight into many-body effects and quantum capacitance in oxide interfaces.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
